mapping.a_element: cosmic expansion rate and its acceleration (driven by cosmological parameters)
mapping.b_element: de Sitter spacetime geometry with residual curvature (cosmological constant Λ)

mapping.a_element: cosmological constant as a driver of cosmic acceleration
mapping.b_element: cosmological constant Λ as a universal constant defining spacetime curvature

mapping.a_element: measurement of luminosity distances (Type Ia supernovae) to probe expansion
mapping.b_element: kinematical group SO(1,4) as the symmetry group of spacetime with Λ

mapping.a_element: theoretical framework for interpreting expansion (e.g., general relativity with dark energy)
mapping.b_element: de Sitter relativity as a framework for special relativity with Λ

brief_justification: While both papers involve the cosmological constant Λ and its role in cosmic expansion or spacetime geometry, Paper A focuses on empirical mapping of expansion history using observational probes (e.g., supernovae, BAO) and theoretical modeling of growth of structure, whereas Paper B constructs a symmetry-based kinematical framework for special relativity in de Sitter spacetime. The underlying methods—observational cosmology vs. group-theoretic kinematics—are distinct, despite shared terminology.
